items.q: What is the central principle of situation ethics?
items.correct: The only intrinsically good thing is agape love; rules can be broken if doing so produces the most loving outcome in the situation.
items.misconception: You should do whatever feels right in the moment.
items.random_wrong: Situation ethics says rules should always be followed.
items.why: Situation ethics is principled (love), not just intuition or relativism. Students collapse it into 'whatever feels right' — Fletcher's actual theory is more disciplined than that.

items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: What does Kant's categorical imperative require?
items.correct: Acting only on maxims you could will to be universal laws, and treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as means.
items.misconception: Treat others how you would like to be treated.
items.random_wrong: Do whatever your conscience tells you to do.
items.why: The Golden Rule depends on your personal preferences (Kant explicitly rejected it). The categorical imperative tests universalisability — whether the maxim can coherently be a law for everyone.

items.q: Does Kant's ethics take consequences into account?
items.correct: No — for Kant the moral worth of an action lies in its being done from duty according to a universalisable maxim; good or bad outcomes neither add nor subtract moral worth.
items.misconception: Kant says an action is right if it produces good consequences for the majority.
items.random_wrong: Kant says actions are right when they make the agent happy.
items.why: Kant is the paradigm deontologist: the good will 'shines like a jewel' even when it achieves nothing. Students smuggle consequentialist reasoning into Kant essays and lose accuracy marks.

items.q: Is Christian ethics simply a matter of obeying the Ten Commandments?
items.correct: No — Christian ethics encompasses Jesus' love commands, the Sermon on the Mount, virtue, conscience, natural law and grace; the Decalogue is one strand within a much richer tradition.
items.misconception: Christian ethics is just the Ten Commandments and nothing else.
items.random_wrong: Christian ethics rejects the Old Testament entirely.
items.why: Jesus summarised the law as love of God and neighbour and deepened its demands (e.g. on anger and lust). Reducing Christian ethics to ten rules misses agape, discipleship and the role of grace.
The answer examiners want is: 1785. “1781” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the second formulation about?
items.why: It's Humanity as end. “Universalisability” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many formulations of the Categorical Imperative?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 formulations. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
